comments.text: The follow-up question doesn't make very much sense, as it is false for $n=4$: two equilateral triangles joined by an edge is the only set which achieves $5$ unit distances. We know exactly how many non-isomorphic graphs achieve an extremal number of unit distances for small $n$, see the commentary at the bottom of this page. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The commentary of that page does not seem to give the count of nonisomorphic constructions, but Table 2 in [Arxiv 2406.15317] indeed indicates that for $3<n\le 15$ (when $u(n)$, the maximum number of unit distances with $n$ points, is known), often there is only one such set up to isomorphism. It may well be that for particular values of $n$, the extremal construction is essentially unique, so it is not even always $>1$ from some value onwards. Nevertheless, answering the question rigorous may be hard, since we even do not know $u(n)$ in general.

$u(n)$ is now known up to $n=21$. The full set of graphs is also known. See the table at the end of "The Erdős unit distance problem for small point sets" by Alexeev, Mixon, Parshall. We only count the configurations up to *graph* isomorphism, but our techniques would allow us to count configurations up to (Euclidean) congruence instead. The case $n=20$ has a unique graph, but I do not remember offhand whether it is also unique in the point set congruence sense. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
